quiz_format.question: When an eBGP-learned prefix is redistributed into iBGP, what happens to the next-hop attribute by default?

options.text: The next-hop is set to the iBGP speaker's loopback address automatically.
The next-hop remains unchanged, preserving the eBGP peer's address.
The next-hop is replaced with 0.0.0.0 until a route-map overrides it.
The next-hop is removed and must be resolved via IGP recursion only.

quiz_format.explanation: By default, iBGP does **not** change the next-hop attribute when advertising routes learned from eBGP. This means the iBGP peer must have a route to the eBGP next-hop, typically via an IGP. The **next-hop-self** command on the iBGP neighbor statement overrides this behavior.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's core routers peer via iBGP across the data center backbone. A new ISP circuit is brought up on an edge router via eBGP. Internal routers cannot reach the ISP-learned prefixes. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The ISP is advertising with a community that blocks internal propagation.
The edge router is not setting next-hop-self on iBGP advertisements, so internal routers lack a route to the eBGP next-hop.
iBGP requires a full mesh, and one peer is missing.
The ISP is sending a default route that overrides more-specific prefixes.

scenario_format.explanation: When an eBGP route is advertised into iBGP, the **next-hop is preserved** by default. If internal routers have no IGP route to the eBGP peer's address, the prefix is marked as unreachable. Configuring **next-hop-self** on the edge router's iBGP neighbor statements resolves this.

rule_format.statement: iBGP preserves the **eBGP next-hop** by default; always configure **next-hop-self** on edge routers advertising into the iBGP mesh to ensure internal reachability.

question_text: BGP path selection order of operations

quiz_format.question: In Cisco's BGP best-path selection algorithm, which attribute is evaluated first?

options.text: Local Preference
AS Path length
Weight
MED (Multi-Exit Discriminator)

quiz_format.explanation: Cisco's BGP best-path algorithm evaluates **Weight** first because it is a Cisco-proprietary, locally significant attribute. The full order begins: **Weight > Local Preference > Locally originated > AS Path length > Origin type > MED**. Weight is set per-neighbor and never propagated to other routers.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson has dual ISP connections at the primary DC. You want Router-A to prefer ISP-1 for all outbound traffic without affecting any other router's decision. Which attribute should you set?

options.text: Set Local Preference to 200 for ISP-1 routes.
Set Weight to 500 on the ISP-1 neighbor statement on Router-A.
Prepend the AS path for ISP-2 routes globally.
Set MED to 0 for ISP-1 routes.

scenario_format.explanation: **Weight** is locally significant to the router and is never advertised to peers, making it the ideal choice when you want to influence path selection on a single router only. **Local Preference** would propagate to all iBGP peers and change their decisions too.

rule_format.statement: **Weight** is evaluated first in Cisco BGP path selection and is **locally significant** — use it when you need to influence one router's preference without affecting the rest of the iBGP domain.

question_text: BGP Local Preference and traffic engineering

quiz_format.question: What is the default Local Preference value, and how does it influence path selection?

options.text: Default is 0; lower values are preferred.
Default is 100; higher values are preferred.
Default is 100; lower values are preferred.
Default is 200; higher values are preferred.

quiz_format.explanation: **Local Preference** defaults to **100** and a **higher** value is preferred. It is shared among all iBGP peers within an AS, making it the primary tool for influencing outbound path selection across an entire autonomous system.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's network team wants all routers in AS 65000 to prefer ISP-A over ISP-B for outbound traffic to the internet. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Set Weight on every router's ISP-A neighbor statement.
Set Local Preference to 200 on routes received from ISP-A at the edge router; it will propagate via iBGP.
Configure a static default route pointing to ISP-A with a lower AD.
Prepend Ferguson's own AS on ISP-B advertisements inbound.

scenario_format.explanation: **Local Preference** propagates to all iBGP peers, so setting it at the edge is sufficient to steer the entire AS's outbound traffic. **Weight** would only affect the local router, and AS prepending controls **inbound**, not outbound, traffic.

rule_format.statement: **Local Preference** (default 100, higher preferred) propagates across iBGP and is the primary tool for **AS-wide outbound** traffic engineering.

question_text: AS Path prepending for inbound traffic engineering

quiz_format.question: When you prepend your own AS number to BGP advertisements sent to a peer, what is the intended effect?

options.text: It increases the local preference for those routes.
It makes the path appear longer to external peers, discouraging inbound traffic via that link.
It triggers route dampening on the peer.
It prevents the route from being installed in the RIB.

quiz_format.explanation: **AS Path prepending** artificially lengthens the AS path, making the route less attractive compared to a shorter-path alternative. This is a common **inbound traffic engineering** technique. It only works when the remote AS has an alternative, shorter path to your prefixes.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson has two ISP uplinks at the DC. Inbound traffic from the internet is splitting roughly 50/50 but the team wants ISP-A to carry 80% of inbound. What should be configured on the ISP-B peering?

options.text: Set MED to a higher value on ISP-B advertisements.
Prepend Ferguson's AS number 2-3 times on routes advertised to ISP-B.
Increase Local Preference on ISP-B-learned routes.
Apply a weight of 0 to ISP-B's neighbor statement.

scenario_format.explanation: **AS Path prepending** on outbound advertisements to ISP-B makes the path through ISP-B appear longer to the rest of the internet, steering inbound traffic toward the shorter path through ISP-A. **MED** only influences a single neighboring AS and is often ignored.

rule_format.statement: **AS Path prepending** on outbound advertisements lengthens the path seen by the internet, steering **inbound** traffic toward the shorter alternative link.

quiz_format.question: By default, when is the MED attribute compared between BGP routes?

options.text: Always, regardless of the originating AS.
Only between routes received from the same neighboring AS.
Only between iBGP-learned routes.
Only when deterministic MED is disabled.

quiz_format.explanation: By default, Cisco compares **MED** only between paths received from the **same neighboring AS**. The **bgp always-compare-med** command overrides this behavior. MED is a **non-transitive optional** attribute — it is sent to a direct eBGP peer but not passed beyond that AS.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson peers with ISP-X at two different PoPs. ISP-X sets different MED values on prefixes sent to each PoP to influence Ferguson's outbound traffic. Will MED be compared by default?

options.text: No — MED comparison only works within iBGP.
Yes — both routes originate from the same AS, so MED is compared.
No — MED is only evaluated after AS path length.
Yes — MED is always compared regardless of origin.

scenario_format.explanation: Since both routes come from **the same AS** (ISP-X), Cisco's default behavior will compare **MED** values. A **lower MED is preferred**. If routes came from two different ISPs, MED would not be compared unless **bgp always-compare-med** is configured.

rule_format.statement: **MED** (lower preferred) is compared only between routes from the **same neighboring AS** by default; it lets a neighbor influence your outbound path selection across their multiple peering points.

question_text: BGP route reflectors and iBGP scalability

quiz_format.question: What problem does a BGP route reflector solve?

options.text: It eliminates the need for eBGP multi-hop.
It relaxes the iBGP full-mesh requirement by allowing a central router to reflect routes to clients.
It replaces confederations in multi-AS designs.
It provides automatic failover between eBGP peers.

quiz_format.explanation: iBGP requires a **full mesh** of peerings — for N routers, that means N*(N-1)/2 sessions. A **route reflector** breaks this requirement by reflecting routes from clients to other clients. Clients only need to peer with the reflector, dramatically reducing configuration complexity.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's DC core has 12 routers running iBGP. Managing 66 peering sessions has become operationally burdensome. What is the best solution to simplify the topology?

options.text: Migrate all routers to eBGP with private AS numbers.
Deploy two route reflectors; all other routers become RR clients, reducing sessions to 22.
Use static redistribution between routers.
Enable BGP confederations with 12 sub-AS numbers.

scenario_format.explanation: **Route reflectors** allow clients to peer only with the RR, eliminating the full-mesh requirement. Deploying **two RRs** provides redundancy. Each client needs only two sessions (one to each RR), vastly simplifying the iBGP topology from **66 to approximately 22 sessions**.

rule_format.statement: **Route reflectors** eliminate the iBGP full-mesh requirement; deploy **redundant RRs** and make all other routers clients to scale iBGP without exponential session growth.

quiz_format.question: Which well-known BGP community tells a receiving router not to advertise the route to any eBGP peer?

options.text: no-export
no-advertise
local-AS
internet

quiz_format.explanation: The **no-export** community prevents a route from being advertised to eBGP peers, keeping it within the local AS (or confederation). **No-advertise** is stricter — it prevents advertisement to any peer, including iBGP. **Local-AS** keeps the route within the local sub-AS in a confederation.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson receives an aggregate from an MPLS provider but should not readvertise it to the public internet ISP. The route must still be visible to all iBGP routers internally. Which community should be applied?

options.text: no-advertise — blocks all advertisements.
no-export — blocks eBGP advertisement while allowing iBGP propagation.
local-AS — restricts the route to a confederation sub-AS.
A custom community with a deny route-map on every eBGP neighbor.

scenario_format.explanation: **No-export** ensures the route is shared across all iBGP peers but is never sent to an eBGP neighbor. This cleanly prevents Ferguson from accidentally becoming a transit AS for the MPLS provider's prefix toward the public internet.

rule_format.statement: **no-export** keeps a route within your AS by blocking eBGP advertisements; **no-advertise** blocks all advertisements including iBGP — choose based on whether internal visibility is needed.

quiz_format.question: What is the primary benefit of BGP Graceful Restart during a control-plane failover?

options.text: It immediately reconverges by flushing the RIB.
It preserves the forwarding table so data-plane traffic continues while the control plane restarts.
It triggers BFD to switch to a backup path.
It prevents route flaps by suppressing all BGP updates for 60 seconds.

quiz_format.explanation: **BGP Graceful Restart** (GR) allows the restarting router to maintain its **forwarding table** during a control-plane restart. The peer (helper) marks routes as stale but continues forwarding. This enables **Non-Stop Forwarding (NSF)**, minimizing traffic disruption during supervisor failovers.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's core router has dual supervisors. During a supervisor switchover, the BGP sessions tear down and reconvergence causes a 90-second outage across branch WAN circuits. What feature should be enabled?

options.text: BFD with aggressive timers to detect failure faster.
BGP Graceful Restart with NSF to preserve forwarding during the switchover.
BGP fast-external-fallover to accelerate reconvergence.
Reduce BGP hold timers to 10 seconds.

scenario_format.explanation: **Graceful Restart with NSF** tells the BGP peer to keep its routes in a stale state and continue forwarding during the switchover. Once the new supervisor initializes, BGP re-establishes and refreshes routes **without dropping data-plane traffic**.

rule_format.statement: **BGP Graceful Restart** with **NSF** preserves the forwarding table during control-plane restarts, preventing data-plane outages on dual-supervisor platforms.

question_text: BGP peer troubleshooting — stuck in Active state

quiz_format.question: A BGP session is stuck in the Active state. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

options.text: The remote peer has a mismatched AS number configured.
The TCP session on port 179 cannot be established due to a connectivity or ACL issue.
The BGP hold timer has expired.
The route reflector cluster-id is misconfigured.

quiz_format.explanation: The **Active** state means BGP is actively trying to establish a TCP connection on **port 179** but failing. Common causes include IP reachability issues, ACL blocking TCP/179, incorrect **update-source** configuration, or TTL issues with eBGP multi-hop. A mismatched AS would result in an **OpenSent** failure, not Active.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's edge router shows a new ISP peering stuck in Active state. Pings to the ISP's peer IP succeed. What should you check next?

options.text: Whether the ISP has a matching route-map applied.
Whether an ACL or firewall rule is blocking TCP port 179, and whether the update-source matches the expected source IP.
Whether the ISP is sending a default route.
Whether the BGP scan timer needs to be reduced.

scenario_format.explanation: A session in **Active** state means TCP cannot connect on port 179. Since ICMP works, the issue is likely a **firewall or ACL blocking TCP/179**, or the source IP of the TCP SYN does not match what the ISP expects (wrong **update-source**). Always verify both ends' neighbor configurations match.

rule_format.statement: BGP stuck in **Active** = TCP/179 cannot connect. Check **ACLs, firewalls, update-source**, and eBGP multi-hop TTL before investigating BGP-layer mismatches.

quiz_format.question: What is the purpose of BGP route dampening?

options.text: To aggregate routes into a single summary prefix.
To suppress routes that flap repeatedly, preventing instability from propagating.
To rate-limit the number of prefixes a peer can advertise.
To prefer routes with the shortest AS path.

quiz_format.explanation: **Route dampening** assigns a penalty to routes each time they flap (withdraw/re-advertise). When the penalty exceeds the **suppress limit**, the route is suppressed. It becomes eligible again when the penalty decays below the **reuse limit**. This prevents unstable routes from causing continuous reconvergence.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's ISP uplink is on an unstable last-mile circuit that flaps every few minutes. Each flap causes BGP reconvergence across the entire iBGP mesh. How can the impact be contained?

options.text: Enable BFD to detect flaps faster.
Apply BGP route dampening to penalize and suppress the flapping prefixes.
Increase the BGP hold timer to 600 seconds.
Remove the eBGP peering and use a static default route.

scenario_format.explanation: **Route dampening** suppresses repeatedly flapping routes so the instability does not ripple through the iBGP mesh. The flapping prefix gets a **penalty** on each withdrawal; once it exceeds the **suppress limit**, BGP stops advertising it until the penalty decays. This stabilizes convergence while the circuit issue is resolved.

rule_format.statement: **Route dampening** penalizes flapping routes — when the penalty exceeds the **suppress limit**, the route is withheld until it decays below the **reuse limit**, preventing instability from cascading through the network.

question_text: OSPF area types and stub area behavior

quiz_format.question: In an OSPF totally stubby area, which LSA types are permitted?

options.text: Types 1, 2, and 3.
Types 1, 2, and a single Type 3 default route.
Types 1, 2, 3, and 5.
Only Types 1 and 2.

quiz_format.explanation: A **totally stubby area** blocks Type 3 (summary), Type 4 (ASBR summary), and Type 5 (external) LSAs. The ABR injects a **single Type 3 default route** (0.0.0.0/0) so internal routers can reach external destinations. Only **Type 1 (router) and Type 2 (network) LSAs** are originated within the area.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson wants to minimize the LSDB size at 1,700 branch routers that each connect back to a regional hub. What OSPF area type would be most effective for branch sites?

options.text: Standard area — branches need full routing visibility.
Totally stubby area — blocks external and inter-area LSAs, injecting only a default route.
NSSA — allows branches to redistribute local routes.
Backbone area 0 — all branches should be in the backbone for fastest convergence.

scenario_format.explanation: With 1,700 branches, LSDB size and SPF computation time are concerns. A **totally stubby area** minimizes the LSDB to local LSAs plus a default route. Since branches typically route all traffic to the hub, they don't need inter-area or external routes.

rule_format.statement: Use **totally stubby areas** for stub branches — they reduce the LSDB to Types 1 and 2 plus a **single default route**, minimizing memory and SPF overhead on remote routers.

quiz_format.question: Which statement about OSPF DR/BDR election on a broadcast segment is correct?

options.text: The DR election is preemptive — a higher-priority router will immediately become DR.
The DR election is non-preemptive — the current DR stays until it fails, even if a higher-priority router joins.
DR election occurs on point-to-point links.
A priority of 255 always forces a new election.

quiz_format.explanation: OSPF DR/BDR election is **non-preemptive**. Once a DR is elected, it remains DR until its interface goes down, even if a router with a higher priority joins the segment. To force a new election, the OSPF process must be restarted on the current DR. **Priority 0** prevents a router from becoming DR/BDR.

scenario_format.question: At a Ferguson DC, a new high-performance router is added to an existing VLAN running OSPF. Despite having the highest priority, it becomes a DROther. Why?

options.text: Its router-ID is lower than the current DR.
OSPF DR election is non-preemptive; the existing DR will hold the role until it fails.
The network type is configured as point-to-multipoint.
The new router's OSPF process ID does not match.

scenario_format.explanation: OSPF DR election is **non-preemptive**, meaning the incumbent DR retains its role regardless of new routers with higher priority. To force the desired outcome, you would need to **restart OSPF on the current DR** or schedule a maintenance window to reset adjacencies.

rule_format.statement: OSPF DR/BDR election is **non-preemptive** — a higher-priority router joining the segment will not displace the existing DR until it fails or OSPF is cleared.

quiz_format.question: Two OSPF routers on the same subnet are stuck in the ExStart/Exchange state. What is the most common cause?

options.text: Mismatched OSPF hello timers.
Mismatched MTU values on the connecting interfaces.
Mismatched OSPF process IDs.
Duplicate router IDs.

quiz_format.explanation: An adjacency stuck in **ExStart/Exchange** typically indicates an **MTU mismatch**. During DBD (Database Description) exchange, OSPF includes the interface MTU; if it doesn't match, the neighbor rejects the packet. Mismatched **hello/dead timers** would prevent the neighbor from reaching Two-Way state at all.

scenario_format.question: A Ferguson branch router was upgraded and its WAN interface is stuck in ExStart with the hub router. Hellos are exchanged successfully. What should you check?

options.text: The OSPF area configuration on both sides.
The MTU settings on both WAN interfaces — a mismatch blocks DBD exchange.
Whether passive-interface is configured on the hub.
Whether the branch is in a stub area.

scenario_format.explanation: Since hellos succeed (reaching Two-Way), the issue is in **DBD exchange**. The most common cause is an **MTU mismatch** — often introduced when one side is reconfigured with a different MTU (e.g., 1500 vs 9000 or a tunneled MTU). The command **ip ospf mtu-ignore** can work around it, but fixing the MTU is the proper solution.

rule_format.statement: OSPF stuck in **ExStart/Exchange** = almost always an **MTU mismatch**. Hellos succeed (timers and area match), but DBD packets are rejected because the advertised MTU doesn't agree.

question_text: OSPF network types and timer defaults

quiz_format.question: What are the default OSPF hello and dead timers on a broadcast network type?

options.text: Hello 30s, Dead 120s.
Hello 10s, Dead 40s.
Hello 5s, Dead 20s.
Hello 10s, Dead 30s.

quiz_format.explanation: On **broadcast** and **point-to-point** network types, the default hello timer is **10 seconds** and the dead timer is **40 seconds** (4x hello). On **NBMA** and **point-to-multipoint non-broadcast** types, the defaults are **30/120 seconds**. Timer mismatches prevent neighbor formation.

scenario_format.question: A Ferguson branch connects to the hub via a Frame Relay link configured as OSPF point-to-point on the hub but NBMA on the branch. Neighbors never form. What is the first thing to verify?

options.text: That the OSPF areas match on both sides.
That the hello and dead timers match — point-to-point uses 10/40s while NBMA uses 30/120s.
That DR/BDR election is configured correctly.
That the OSPF cost is the same on both interfaces.

scenario_format.explanation: Different OSPF **network types** have different default timers. **Point-to-point** defaults to 10/40s while **NBMA** defaults to 30/120s. Since these don't match, hellos are never acknowledged within the dead interval. The fix is to make the **network types consistent** on both ends.

rule_format.statement: OSPF **broadcast/point-to-point** defaults: Hello 10s, Dead 40s. **NBMA**: Hello 30s, Dead 120s. Mismatched network types cause **timer mismatches** that silently prevent adjacency formation.

quiz_format.question: When is an OSPF virtual link required?

options.text: When two routers in the same area cannot form an adjacency.
When a non-backbone area does not have a direct physical connection to area 0.
When the DR on a segment fails.
When redistributing external routes into OSPF.

quiz_format.explanation: OSPF requires all areas to connect to **area 0** (backbone). If an area is disconnected from area 0 due to network design, a **virtual link** can extend the backbone through a transit area. Virtual links are configured between two **ABRs** — one on the disconnected area's border and one on area 0's border.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson acquires a regional distributor whose OSPF area 10 connects to Ferguson's area 20 but has no direct link to area 0. How can inter-area routing be restored?

options.text: Redistribute area 10 routes into area 0 using a route-map.
Configure a virtual link through area 20 to extend area 0 to the ABR serving area 10.
Convert area 10 into a stub area.
Add a static route on all area 0 routers pointing to area 10 prefixes.

scenario_format.explanation: A **virtual link** through the transit area (area 20) logically extends the backbone to the ABR connecting area 10. This is the correct OSPF mechanism for areas that cannot directly connect to **area 0**. Virtual links are a temporary design fix — a physical link to area 0 is preferred long-term.

rule_format.statement: Every OSPF area must connect to **area 0**. When physical connectivity is impossible, a **virtual link** through a transit area extends the backbone — but treat it as a temporary fix, not a design goal.

question_text: OSPF NSSA and Type 7 LSA translation

quiz_format.question: What distinguishes an NSSA from a standard stub area?

options.text: NSSA allows Type 5 external LSAs.
NSSA permits redistribution into OSPF via Type 7 LSAs, which the ABR translates to Type 5.
NSSA blocks all inter-area routes.
NSSA does not require an ABR.

quiz_format.explanation: An **NSSA (Not-So-Stubby Area)** blocks Type 5 LSAs like a stub area but allows local redistribution using **Type 7 LSAs**. The ABR then translates these Type 7 LSAs into **Type 5** for flooding into the rest of the OSPF domain. This is useful when a remote site has locally connected networks that need to be redistributed.

scenario_format.question: A Ferguson branch office runs a local POS system on a directly connected network that must be advertised into OSPF. The branch is in a stub area to minimize LSDB size. The POS subnet cannot be advertised. What change is needed?

options.text: Convert the stub area to a standard area.
Convert the stub area to an NSSA, allowing the POS subnet to be redistributed as a Type 7 LSA.
Add a static route for the POS subnet on the hub.
Move the POS subnet interface into area 0.

scenario_format.explanation: An **NSSA** allows local redistribution using **Type 7 LSAs** while still blocking external Type 5 LSAs from entering the area. The ABR translates the Type 7 to Type 5 for the rest of the OSPF domain. This provides the stub area benefits while permitting local route injection for the POS network.

rule_format.statement: **NSSA** = stub area + local redistribution. External routes enter as **Type 7 LSAs**, translated to **Type 5** at the ABR — ideal for branches that need stub benefits but have locally redistributed networks.

question_text: OSPF SPF throttle timers and convergence tuning

quiz_format.question: What do the three OSPF SPF throttle timer values (spf-start, spf-hold, spf-max-wait) control?

options.text: The intervals between hello packets during convergence.
The initial delay before the first SPF run, the hold time between subsequent runs, and the maximum wait time before resetting.
The time to wait before declaring a neighbor dead.
The LSA flooding interval on broadcast segments.

quiz_format.explanation: OSPF SPF throttle uses three values: **spf-start** (initial delay before the first SPF calculation), **spf-hold** (minimum interval between subsequent SPF runs, doubling with each trigger), and **spf-max-wait** (maximum interval cap). Tuning these values balances **fast convergence** against **CPU protection** during instability.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's core routers experience high CPU during OSPF reconvergence events when multiple branch circuits flap simultaneously. What tuning approach helps?

options.text: Reduce the hello timer to 1 second for faster detection.
Tune SPF throttle timers — set a short spf-start for fast initial convergence and increase spf-hold/spf-max-wait to dampen repeated calculations.
Increase the reference bandwidth to 100 Gbps.
Disable SPF and rely on distance-vector behavior.

scenario_format.explanation: **SPF throttle timers** (e.g., 5 50 5000 ms) allow the first SPF to run quickly (5 ms) while exponentially backing off during repeated triggers. This protects CPU during **mass flap events** while still providing sub-second convergence for the initial failure.

rule_format.statement: Tune **SPF throttle timers** with a short **spf-start** (fast first convergence) and progressive **spf-hold/spf-max-wait** backoff to protect CPU during mass reconvergence events.

question_text: OSPF adjacency states and Full state

quiz_format.question: In what order do OSPF adjacency states progress from initialization to full adjacency?

options.text: Down, Init, Two-Way, ExStart, Exchange, Loading, Full.
Down, Init, Exchange, ExStart, Two-Way, Loading, Full.
Down, Two-Way, Init, ExStart, Loading, Exchange, Full.
Down, Init, Two-Way, Loading, Exchange, ExStart, Full.

quiz_format.explanation: The correct OSPF state progression is: **Down, Init, Two-Way, ExStart, Exchange, Loading, Full**. Two-Way is where DR/BDR election occurs on broadcast segments. ExStart negotiates the master/slave relationship. Exchange is where DBDs are swapped. Loading fetches missing LSAs.

scenario_format.question: While troubleshooting a Ferguson branch adjacency, you see the neighbor in Two-Way state on a point-to-point link. Is this normal?

options.text: Yes — Two-Way is the stable state for DROther routers.
No — on a point-to-point link, all neighbors should reach Full state; Two-Way indicates a problem progressing to ExStart.
Yes — Two-Way means routing is functional.
No — Two-Way only occurs on NBMA networks.

scenario_format.explanation: On **point-to-point** links, there is no DR/BDR election; all neighbors should progress to **Full**. Two-Way is only a normal stable state for **DROther** routers on broadcast segments. A neighbor stuck at Two-Way on a point-to-point link suggests something is preventing the **ExStart/Exchange** process.

rule_format.statement: On **point-to-point** links, all OSPF neighbors must reach **Full** state. Two-Way as a stable state is only normal for **DROther** pairs on broadcast segments.

question_text: OSPF reference bandwidth and cost calculation

quiz_format.question: The default OSPF reference bandwidth is 100 Mbps. What problem does this create for modern networks?

options.text: It assigns a cost of 0 to all links.
All interfaces 100 Mbps and faster receive the same cost of 1, making OSPF unable to distinguish between Fast Ethernet, Gigabit, and 10 Gigabit links.
It causes SPF to run continuously.
It prevents redistribution from BGP.

quiz_format.explanation: OSPF cost = **reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth**. With the default 100 Mbps reference, FastEthernet (100 Mbps), GigE (1 Gbps), and 10 GigE all calculate to a cost of **1** (integer rounding). Using **auto-cost reference-bandwidth** with a higher value (e.g., 100000 for 100 Gbps) restores meaningful cost differentiation.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's DC has 10 GigE and 100 GigE links, but OSPF treats them identically. Traffic takes suboptimal paths through slower links. What should be configured?

options.text: Manually set ip ospf cost on every interface.
Set auto-cost reference-bandwidth to 100000 (100 Gbps) on all routers in the OSPF domain.
Change all links to point-to-point network type.
Enable OSPF fast-reroute to prefer higher-bandwidth paths.

scenario_format.explanation: Setting **auto-cost reference-bandwidth 100000** makes 10 GigE cost 10 and 100 GigE cost 1, restoring meaningful differentiation. This must be configured on **all routers** in the OSPF domain to maintain consistent cost calculations and prevent routing loops.

rule_format.statement: Set **auto-cost reference-bandwidth** to your highest link speed across **all OSPF routers** — the default 100 Mbps collapses all modern link speeds to the same cost of 1.

quiz_format.question: Which OSPF authentication type provides the strongest protection against spoofed OSPF packets?

options.text: Type 0 — null authentication.
Type 1 — plaintext password.
Type 2 — MD5 cryptographic authentication.
Type 3 — SHA-256 authentication.

quiz_format.explanation: OSPF supports **Type 0 (null)**, **Type 1 (plaintext)**, and **Type 2 (MD5)**. MD5 authentication provides **cryptographic hashing** of OSPF packets, preventing spoofing and replay attacks. Plaintext sends the password in the clear. Newer IOS versions support **SHA-256** via the key-chain mechanism, but the standard types are 0, 1, and 2.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's security team requires OSPF authentication on all WAN-facing interfaces to prevent rogue route injection at branch sites. What is the recommended implementation?

options.text: Type 1 plaintext authentication — simple to manage.
Type 2 MD5 authentication with area-level enforcement to ensure all routers authenticate.
Passive-interface on all WAN interfaces.
ACL filtering of OSPF multicast (224.0.0.5/6).

scenario_format.explanation: **MD5 authentication** (Type 2) ensures all OSPF packets are cryptographically verified. Configuring it at the **area level** forces all interfaces in that area to authenticate, preventing an engineer from accidentally leaving an interface unprotected. This is a key control for **SOX compliance** at a company like Ferguson.

rule_format.statement: Deploy **OSPF MD5 authentication** (Type 2) at the area level on all WAN-facing segments — it prevents rogue route injection and supports **change control/compliance** requirements.

question_text: DMVPN Phase 2 vs Phase 3 spoke-to-spoke behavior

quiz_format.question: What is the primary difference between DMVPN Phase 2 and Phase 3 for spoke-to-spoke communication?

options.text: Phase 2 does not support spoke-to-spoke tunnels at all.
Phase 3 uses NHRP redirect and shortcut to allow spoke-to-spoke tunnels even with summarized routing, while Phase 2 requires specific routes.
Phase 3 encrypts traffic while Phase 2 does not.
Phase 3 eliminates the need for a hub.

quiz_format.explanation: In **Phase 2**, spoke-to-spoke tunnels require the spoke to have a specific route for the destination — summarization at the hub breaks spoke-to-spoke. **Phase 3** introduces **NHRP redirect** (hub-side) and **NHRP shortcut** (spoke-side), allowing the hub to redirect spokes to build direct tunnels even when the hub advertises only summary routes.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson runs DMVPN with 1,700 branch spokes. The hub advertises a summary route for all branch subnets. Branches need to send voice traffic directly to each other without hairpinning through the hub. Which DMVPN phase is required?

options.text: Phase 1 with static NHRP mappings.
Phase 3 with NHRP redirect on the hub and NHRP shortcut on spokes.
Phase 2 with full routing tables on every spoke.
Phase 1 with GRE keepalives.

scenario_format.explanation: With summarized routing at the hub, **Phase 3** is required. The hub sends an **NHRP redirect** to the source spoke, which installs an **NHRP shortcut** and builds a direct tunnel to the destination spoke. This enables spoke-to-spoke communication without carrying 1,700 specific routes on every spoke.

rule_format.statement: **DMVPN Phase 3** = summary routing at the hub + spoke-to-spoke tunnels via **NHRP redirect/shortcut**. Phase 2 requires specific routes on spokes, which doesn't scale to 1,700 branches.

quiz_format.question: What is the role of NHRP in a DMVPN deployment?

options.text: It encrypts tunnel traffic between spokes.
It maps tunnel (logical) addresses to NBMA (physical/underlay) addresses, enabling dynamic tunnel creation.
It replaces the IGP routing protocol inside the tunnel.
It performs QoS classification on tunnel packets.

quiz_format.explanation: **NHRP (Next Hop Resolution Protocol)** provides the mapping between **tunnel IP addresses** and **NBMA (underlay) addresses** (i.e., the public IPs of the GRE endpoints). Spokes register their mappings with the hub (NHS), and resolution requests allow spokes to discover each other's physical addresses for direct tunnel creation.

scenario_format.question: A new Ferguson branch spoke registers with the DMVPN hub but cannot build spoke-to-spoke tunnels. NHRP resolution replies from the hub contain stale mappings. What should you check?

options.text: Whether the IPSec transform set matches.
Whether the NHRP registration timeout and holdtime are too long, causing stale entries to persist after spoke IP changes.
Whether the spoke is in Phase 1.
Whether the hub's GRE tunnel key matches.

scenario_format.explanation: If a spoke's underlay IP changes (e.g., ISP re-assignment), the hub's **NHRP cache** may hold the old mapping until the **registration timer** expires. Reducing the **NHRP holdtime** ensures stale entries are purged faster. The command **ip nhrp holdtime** controls this on the spoke, and **ip nhrp registration timeout** controls re-registration frequency.

rule_format.statement: **NHRP** maps tunnel IPs to underlay IPs — tune **holdtime** and **registration timeout** to ensure stale mappings are purged quickly when branch ISP addresses change.

quiz_format.question: What is the advantage of using 'tunnel protection ipsec profile' over a traditional crypto map in DMVPN?

options.text: It provides stronger encryption algorithms.
It dynamically encrypts all GRE tunnel traffic without needing ACL-based crypto maps, and supports spoke-to-spoke SA creation.
It eliminates the need for IKE negotiation.
It replaces NHRP for address resolution.

quiz_format.explanation: **Tunnel protection** applies an IPSec profile directly to the GRE tunnel interface, encrypting all traffic through it without crypto map ACLs. It automatically creates **IPSec SAs** for spoke-to-spoke tunnels as NHRP resolves them. This is simpler and more scalable than managing crypto maps with explicit peer definitions.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson is migrating from legacy site-to-site VPNs with crypto maps to DMVPN. The team wants to ensure all branch-to-branch traffic is encrypted without managing individual crypto ACLs for 1,700 sites. What approach is recommended?

options.text: Create a crypto map with 1,700 peer entries.
Use tunnel protection with an IPSec profile on the mGRE interface — SAs are created dynamically as NHRP resolves spoke addresses.
Rely on GRE encapsulation alone for confidentiality.
Deploy a separate VPN concentrator for encryption.

scenario_format.explanation: **Tunnel protection ipsec profile** on the mGRE interface provides automatic encryption for all DMVPN traffic. **IPSec SAs** are built dynamically when NHRP resolves a spoke's address, eliminating the need to define crypto maps or ACLs for each of 1,700 peers.

rule_format.statement: Use **tunnel protection ipsec profile** on DMVPN mGRE interfaces — it dynamically creates IPSec SAs as NHRP resolves peers, eliminating per-site crypto map management.

quiz_format.question: In a DMVPN dual-hub design, how should spokes be configured for redundancy?

options.text: Each spoke configures two tunnel interfaces, one to each hub, with routing metrics to control primary/backup preference.
Spokes configure a single tunnel with two NHRP servers and automatic failover.
The backup hub is configured as a spoke and promotes itself during failure.
Dual hubs share a virtual IP via HSRP on the tunnel interface.

quiz_format.explanation: The standard approach uses **two tunnel interfaces** on each spoke, each pointing to a different hub as the **NHS (Next Hop Server)**. The IGP (typically EIGRP or OSPF) controls path preference through **metrics or administrative distance**. During a hub failure, the routing protocol converges to the backup tunnel.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson deploys DMVPN hubs in the primary and DR data centers. If the primary DC hub fails, all 1,700 branches must failover. What is the recommended design?

options.text: Configure each spoke with two tunnel interfaces (Tunnel0 to primary hub, Tunnel1 to DR hub) and use EIGRP with higher delay on Tunnel1 for backup preference.
Use a single tunnel with NHRP pointing to both hubs simultaneously.
Deploy VRRP between the two hubs on the tunnel interface.
Rely on ISP failover rather than DMVPN redundancy.

scenario_format.explanation: **Dual tunnel interfaces** with the routing protocol controlling preference is the proven DMVPN redundancy model. Setting **higher EIGRP delay** on Tunnel1 (DR hub) makes it the backup. When the primary hub fails, EIGRP converges to the DR hub tunnel within seconds across all 1,700 branches.

rule_format.statement: DMVPN redundancy = **two tunnel interfaces per spoke**, one per hub, with the **IGP controlling primary/backup** preference via metrics — never rely on a single hub for 1,700 branches.

question_text: DMVPN tunnel key and GRE configuration

quiz_format.question: What happens if the GRE tunnel key does not match between a DMVPN hub and spoke?

options.text: The tunnel forms but traffic is not encrypted.
NHRP registration fails because the GRE key is embedded in the tunnel header and packets are silently dropped.
The spoke automatically renegotiates the key.
The tunnel forms with degraded throughput.

quiz_format.explanation: The **GRE tunnel key** is embedded in the GRE header. If the hub and spoke have mismatched keys, the receiving end silently drops the packets because the key does not match its expected value. **NHRP registration** never succeeds, and the spoke appears to be unreachable. This is a common DMVPN misconfiguration.

scenario_format.question: A Ferguson technician deploys a new branch router from a template. The DMVPN tunnel comes up but the spoke cannot register with the hub. Debug NHRP shows no registration requests received at the hub. What is a likely misconfiguration?

options.text: The OSPF area is misconfigured.
The GRE tunnel key on the spoke does not match the hub's key, causing packets to be silently dropped.
The spoke's loopback address is wrong.
The spoke needs a static route to the hub's tunnel IP.

scenario_format.explanation: When the hub receives **zero registration requests**, the issue is at the GRE/transport level, not NHRP. A mismatched **tunnel key** causes the hub to drop the spoke's GRE packets silently. Verify the tunnel key matches on both hub and spoke, along with the tunnel source and tunnel mode gre multipoint configuration.

rule_format.statement: A mismatched **GRE tunnel key** causes silent packet drops — if the hub sees no NHRP registrations, verify the **tunnel key, tunnel source**, and **tunnel mode** before troubleshooting NHRP.

quiz_format.question: Why must EIGRP split-horizon be disabled on the DMVPN hub's tunnel interface?

options.text: To allow the hub to learn routes from all spokes.
To allow the hub to re-advertise spoke routes back out the same mGRE interface to other spokes.
To prevent routing loops between dual hubs.
To enable EIGRP stub routing on spokes.

quiz_format.explanation: **Split-horizon** prevents a router from advertising routes back out the interface on which they were learned. Since all spokes connect to the hub via the **same mGRE interface**, split-horizon would prevent the hub from sharing Spoke-A's routes with Spoke-B. Disabling it with **no ip split-horizon eigrp** is mandatory.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's DMVPN hub runs EIGRP. Branch A can reach the hub but not Branch B, even though both are registered via NHRP. What is the likely issue?

options.text: Branch B's NHRP registration has expired.
EIGRP split-horizon is enabled on the hub's tunnel interface, preventing spoke-to-spoke route advertisements.
Branch B's OSPF area does not match.
The hub is configured for DMVPN Phase 1.

scenario_format.explanation: With **EIGRP split-horizon enabled** (the default), the hub learns Branch B's route on the tunnel interface but cannot advertise it back out the same interface to Branch A. Disabling split-horizon with **no ip split-horizon eigrp [ASN]** on the hub's tunnel interface resolves inter-spoke routing.

rule_format.statement: Always disable **EIGRP split-horizon** on the DMVPN hub's mGRE interface — without it, spoke routes learned on the tunnel cannot be re-advertised to other spokes.

quiz_format.question: A DMVPN tunnel is up (GRE and NHRP work), but traffic is being dropped. 'show crypto ipsec sa' shows zero encrypted/decrypted packets. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The NHRP network-id does not match.
The IKE or IPSec proposal (transform set, DH group, or pre-shared key) is mismatched between peers, preventing SA establishment.
The tunnel MTU is too high.
EIGRP is not advertising the spoke's networks.

quiz_format.explanation: If GRE and NHRP work but the **IPSec SA** shows zero packets, IKE negotiation is failing. Check **Phase 1** (ISAKMP policy: encryption, hash, DH group, authentication method) and **Phase 2** (transform set: encryption and integrity algorithms). A **pre-shared key mismatch** is the most frequent cause in branch deployments.

scenario_format.question: A newly deployed Ferguson branch can ping the hub's tunnel IP but all production traffic is dropped. The tunnel protection IPSec profile shows no active SAs. What is the first troubleshooting step?

options.text: Check whether the branch's ISP is blocking GRE.
Verify the IKE policy and pre-shared key match between the spoke and hub — a mismatch prevents IPSec SA formation.
Restart OSPF on the branch router.
Increase the tunnel MTU.

scenario_format.explanation: The spoke can reach the hub (pings work over GRE), but the **IPSec layer** is not encrypting. This means IKE negotiation is failing. Verify the **ISAKMP policy** (encryption, hash, lifetime, DH group) and **pre-shared key** match on both ends. Use **debug crypto isakmp** to see the specific mismatch.

rule_format.statement: GRE works + IPSec SA shows zero packets = **IKE negotiation failure**. Check the **ISAKMP policy, transform set, and pre-shared key** match between hub and spoke.

quiz_format.question: What is the key limitation of DMVPN Phase 1?

options.text: It does not support IPSec encryption.
All spoke-to-spoke traffic must traverse the hub because spokes cannot build direct tunnels.
It cannot scale beyond 50 spokes.
It requires static routing on all spokes.

quiz_format.explanation: **DMVPN Phase 1** is a hub-and-spoke-only topology. Spokes register with the hub but cannot resolve other spokes' NBMA addresses, so all inter-spoke traffic **hairpins through the hub**. This is the simplest phase but creates a bandwidth bottleneck at the hub for spoke-to-spoke communication.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson initially deployed DMVPN Phase 1. As branch-to-branch voice traffic grows, the hub's WAN bandwidth becomes saturated. What is the root cause and solution?

options.text: The hub needs more CPU — upgrade the router.
Phase 1 forces all spoke-to-spoke traffic through the hub. Migrating to Phase 2 or Phase 3 enables direct spoke-to-spoke tunnels.
Enable QoS on the hub to prioritize voice.
Add more NHRP servers to distribute load.

scenario_format.explanation: In **Phase 1**, spoke-to-spoke traffic traverses the hub, doubling bandwidth consumption. Migrating to **Phase 3** (preferred at scale) allows spokes to build **direct tunnels** via NHRP shortcuts, offloading the hub. Phase 3 also supports route summarization, critical for 1,700 branches.

rule_format.statement: **DMVPN Phase 1** = hub-and-spoke only; all spoke-to-spoke traffic **hairpins through the hub**. Migrate to **Phase 3** for direct spoke-to-spoke tunnels with summarized routing at scale.

quiz_format.question: In an MPLS L3 VPN architecture, which router maintains VRF instances and exchanges routes with the customer?

options.text: The P (Provider) router.
The PE (Provider Edge) router.
The CE (Customer Edge) router.
The route reflector.

quiz_format.explanation: The **PE (Provider Edge)** router is where customer VRFs are configured. It peers with the CE via eBGP, OSPF, or static routes and uses **MP-BGP** to exchange VPNv4 routes with other PEs. The **P (Provider)** router only performs label switching — it has no knowledge of customer routes. The **CE** is the customer's router.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson connects to an MPLS provider at each branch. Ferguson's branch router peers via eBGP with the provider's router that holds Ferguson's VRF. What role does the provider's router play?

options.text: P router — it performs label switching.
PE router — it maintains the customer VRF and exchanges routes with the CE.
CE router — it connects to the customer.
ASBR — it connects two provider networks.

scenario_format.explanation: The provider's router that holds Ferguson's **VRF** and peers with Ferguson's branch router (CE) is the **PE router**. It translates between the customer's routing domain and the MPLS provider's **VPNv4** backbone. Ferguson's branch router is the **CE**.

rule_format.statement: **PE** routers hold customer VRFs and bridge between CE routing (eBGP/OSPF) and the MPLS backbone (**MP-BGP VPNv4**). **P** routers only label-switch — they never see customer routes.

question_text: MPLS label operations: push, swap, pop

quiz_format.question: What are the three fundamental MPLS label operations?

options.text: Encapsulate, route, decapsulate.
Push (impose), swap (exchange), and pop (remove).
Tag, forward, untag.
Label, lookup, strip.

quiz_format.explanation: MPLS uses three label operations: **Push** (impose a label onto a packet entering the MPLS domain), **Swap** (exchange one label for another at transit P routers), and **Pop** (remove the label as the packet exits the MPLS domain). **Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)** has the second-to-last router pop the label to reduce the egress PE's workload.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's traffic traverses an MPLS provider backbone. At the second-to-last provider router before the destination PE, the top label is removed. What is this behavior called?

options.text: Label stripping.
Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP) — the second-to-last hop removes the top label so the egress PE doesn't need to perform two lookups.
Label aggregation.
Implicit null tagging.

scenario_format.explanation: **Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)** occurs when the router before the egress PE pops the transport label. The egress PE then only needs to perform an IP lookup (or VPN label lookup) instead of both a label swap and IP lookup. The egress PE signals this by advertising an **implicit null** label to its penultimate hop.

rule_format.statement: MPLS label operations: **push** at ingress, **swap** at transit, **pop** at egress. **PHP** optimizes the egress PE by having the penultimate hop pop the transport label early.

quiz_format.question: What is the purpose of the VPNv4 address family in MP-BGP?

options.text: To encrypt MPLS label traffic between PEs.
To carry customer IPv4 prefixes with a Route Distinguisher, allowing overlapping address spaces between different VPNs.
To replace LDP for label distribution.
To provide IPv4 multicast support in MPLS.

quiz_format.explanation: **VPNv4** prefixes consist of an 8-byte **Route Distinguisher (RD)** prepended to a 4-byte IPv4 prefix, creating a unique 12-byte address. This allows **MP-BGP** to carry routes from multiple customers who may use the same private IP ranges (e.g., 10.0.0.0/8) without conflict. RDs make each prefix globally unique within the provider's network.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson and another provider customer both use 10.0.0.0/8 internally. The MPLS provider carries both customers' routes across the same backbone. How does MP-BGP keep them separate?

options.text: By using different OSPF areas for each customer.
By prepending a unique Route Distinguisher to each customer's prefixes in the VPNv4 address family.
By assigning different MPLS labels to each customer at every hop.
By maintaining separate physical links for each customer.

scenario_format.explanation: The **Route Distinguisher (RD)** makes each customer's prefix unique within the VPNv4 table. Ferguson's 10.0.0.0/8 might become **65000:1:10.0.0.0/8** while the other customer's becomes **65000:2:10.0.0.0/8**. **Route Targets (RTs)** then control which VRFs import/export which prefixes.

rule_format.statement: **VPNv4** = Route Distinguisher + IPv4 prefix. The **RD** makes overlapping addresses unique in MP-BGP; **Route Targets** control which VRFs import/export which routes.

quiz_format.question: What is the difference between a Route Distinguisher (RD) and a Route Target (RT) in MPLS L3 VPN?

options.text: RD determines traffic forwarding priority; RT determines label allocation.
RD makes prefixes unique in the VPNv4 table; RT controls which VRFs import and export routes.
RD is set by the CE; RT is set by the PE.
They are interchangeable and serve the same function.

quiz_format.explanation: The **Route Distinguisher** is a prefix modifier that creates uniqueness in the VPNv4 table — it is purely a disambiguation mechanism. The **Route Target** is a BGP extended community that controls **route import/export policy** between VRFs. Multiple VRFs can share an RT to create shared-service topologies (e.g., hub-and-spoke or full-mesh VPN).

scenario_format.question: Ferguson wants branch VRFs to exchange routes with each other (full-mesh) but also import routes from a shared-services VRF (internet gateway, DNS). How should Route Targets be configured?

options.text: Use the same RD for all VRFs.
Assign one RT for branch-to-branch (import/export on all branch VRFs) and a second RT for shared-services (exported by shared-services VRF, imported by branch VRFs).
Use unique RTs per branch — no overlapping allowed.
Configure static routes instead of RTs for shared services.

scenario_format.explanation: **Route Targets** can be layered. All branch VRFs export/import a common RT (e.g., 65000:100) for full-mesh connectivity. The shared-services VRF exports a second RT (e.g., 65000:999) that all branch VRFs import. This creates a **hub-and-spoke overlay** for shared services on top of the branch full mesh.

rule_format.statement: **RD** = uniqueness (no policy impact). **RT** = route import/export policy. Layer multiple RTs on a VRF to build complex topologies like **full-mesh + shared-services** without route leaking.

quiz_format.question: What protocol is most commonly used to distribute transport labels in an MPLS network?

options.text: MP-BGP.
LDP (Label Distribution Protocol).
RSVP-TE.
OSPF with opaque LSAs.

quiz_format.explanation: **LDP (Label Distribution Protocol)** is the most common protocol for distributing transport labels in an MPLS network. It automatically builds **Label Switched Paths (LSPs)** based on the IGP's routing table. **RSVP-TE** is used when traffic engineering is required. **MP-BGP** distributes VPN labels, not transport labels.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's MPLS provider reports that traffic between two PE routers is being IP-routed instead of label-switched through the P routers. What should the provider check?

options.text: Whether MP-BGP is configured on the P routers.
Whether LDP is enabled on the P router interfaces along the path and LDP sessions are established.
Whether VRFs are configured on the P routers.
Whether the IGP cost is too high.

scenario_format.explanation: If LDP is not enabled on a **P router's interface**, no label is assigned for that hop, breaking the **Label Switched Path**. The packet falls back to IP forwarding at that point. P routers need **LDP** (not MP-BGP or VRFs) to perform label switching. Verify with **show mpls ldp neighbor** and **show mpls forwarding-table**.

rule_format.statement: **LDP** distributes transport labels to build LSPs across P routers. If LDP is missing on any interface in the path, the LSP breaks and traffic falls back to **IP forwarding**.

quiz_format.question: How many labels are typically in an MPLS L3 VPN packet's label stack?

options.text: One — the VPN label.
Two — a transport label (top) and a VPN label (bottom).
Three — transport, VPN, and QoS labels.
One — the transport label only.

quiz_format.explanation: An MPLS L3 VPN packet carries a **two-label stack**. The **top label** (transport/IGP label) is distributed by LDP and gets the packet across the provider backbone. The **bottom label** (VPN label) is distributed by MP-BGP and tells the egress PE which VRF to place the packet into. PHP removes the top label at the penultimate hop.

scenario_format.question: Traffic from a Ferguson branch traverses the MPLS provider and arrives at the egress PE. After PHP, only one label remains. What does this remaining label determine?

options.text: The output interface on the PE.
The VRF into which the packet should be placed for forwarding to the correct customer CE.
The QoS treatment for the packet.
The return path for the packet.

scenario_format.explanation: After **PHP** removes the transport label, the remaining **VPN label** tells the egress PE which **VRF** to use for the IP lookup. This ensures Ferguson's traffic is forwarded to Ferguson's CE, not to another customer's CE. The VPN label is assigned by the egress PE and distributed via **MP-BGP**.

rule_format.statement: MPLS L3 VPN uses a **two-label stack**: the **transport label** (LDP, top) routes across the backbone; the **VPN label** (MP-BGP, bottom) identifies the destination VRF at the egress PE.

quiz_format.question: Which routing protocol is most commonly used between a CE and PE in MPLS L3 VPN deployments, and why?

options.text: OSPF — because the PE can redistribute into MP-BGP.
eBGP — because it provides clean administrative separation between customer and provider routing domains.
RIP — because it is simplest.
IS-IS — because it supports MPLS natively.

quiz_format.explanation: **eBGP** is preferred for CE-PE peering because it naturally separates the customer's routing domain from the provider's. It supports rich policy control, scales well, and avoids the complexity of mutual redistribution (unlike OSPF or EIGRP CE-PE peering). It also allows the PE to carry customer routes natively in **VPNv4** without additional redistribution.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's MPLS provider offers eBGP or OSPF for the CE-PE peering at each branch. With 1,700 branches and the need for consistent policy, which should Ferguson choose?

options.text: OSPF — it matches Ferguson's internal IGP.
eBGP — it provides clean domain separation, consistent policy via route-maps, and avoids complex redistribution.
Static routes — simplest at scale.
EIGRP — best for Cisco-to-Cisco environments.

scenario_format.explanation: **eBGP** CE-PE peering gives Ferguson control via **route-maps and prefix-lists** without the complexity of redistributing between the provider's OSPF and MP-BGP. At 1,700 branches, consistent policy application and clean administrative boundaries make eBGP the operationally superior choice.

rule_format.statement: Use **eBGP** for CE-PE peering in MPLS L3 VPN — it provides clean administrative separation, avoids redistribution complexity, and enables consistent policy at scale via **route-maps**.

quiz_format.question: Why might a standard IP traceroute fail to show all hops through an MPLS provider's backbone?

options.text: MPLS routers do not decrement TTL by default.
P routers may not propagate the IP TTL into the MPLS label header, causing the MPLS hops to be invisible.
MPLS blocks ICMP at all P routers.
Traceroute does not work with label switching.

quiz_format.explanation: By default on many platforms, MPLS does **not propagate the IP TTL** into the MPLS header (or vice versa). This means P routers never see the IP TTL expire, so they never send ICMP Time Exceeded replies. The command **mpls ip propagate-ttl** controls this behavior. Providers often disable propagation intentionally to hide their topology.

scenario_format.question: A Ferguson engineer runs traceroute to a remote branch through the MPLS WAN. The output shows the local CE, then the remote CE — no intermediate hops. Is this expected?

options.text: No — there is a routing problem bypassing the provider backbone.
Yes — the provider likely disables TTL propagation, hiding MPLS backbone hops from customer traceroutes.
No — MPLS should show all label-switched hops.
Yes — MPLS replaces IP routing so hops are not visible.

scenario_format.explanation: Most MPLS providers **disable IP TTL propagation** into the MPLS domain for security (topology hiding). Customer traceroutes only show the CE hops on each end. To troubleshoot within the MPLS cloud, the provider must use **MPLS traceroute (LSP traceroute)** from within their network.

rule_format.statement: Providers commonly disable **TTL propagation** into MPLS, hiding backbone hops from customer traceroutes. Use **MPLS LSP traceroute** from within the provider network to troubleshoot path issues.

question_text: DSCP markings for voice and video

quiz_format.question: What DSCP value is recommended for voice bearer traffic (RTP) per the RFC 4594 guidelines?

options.text: AF41 (DSCP 34).
EF (DSCP 46).
CS5 (DSCP 40).
AF31 (DSCP 26).

quiz_format.explanation: **EF (Expedited Forwarding, DSCP 46)** is the standard marking for voice bearer (RTP) traffic. It receives **strict priority queuing** (Low Latency Queuing) to ensure minimal delay, jitter, and loss. Voice signaling (SIP/H.323) is typically marked **CS3 (DSCP 24)** and placed in a guaranteed bandwidth queue.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson deploys VoIP phones at 1,700 branches over WAN circuits. Voice quality is poor during peak hours. Which DSCP marking and queuing treatment should voice RTP traffic receive?

options.text: AF41 with weighted fair queuing.
EF (DSCP 46) with strict priority queuing (LLQ), allocated no more than 33% of link bandwidth.
CS1 with best-effort queuing.
DSCP 0 with traffic shaping.

scenario_format.explanation: Voice RTP must be marked **EF** and placed in a **Low Latency Queue (LLQ)** with strict priority. Best practice limits the LLQ to **33% of link bandwidth** to prevent priority traffic from starving other queues. Voice requires less than **150 ms one-way delay**, **< 30 ms jitter**, and **< 1% packet loss**.

rule_format.statement: Voice RTP = **EF (DSCP 46)** in a **strict priority (LLQ)** queue, capped at ~33% of link bandwidth. Target: < 150 ms delay, < 30 ms jitter, < 1% loss.

quiz_format.question: What is the fundamental difference between traffic policing and traffic shaping?

options.text: Policing buffers excess traffic; shaping drops it.
Policing drops or re-marks excess traffic immediately; shaping buffers it and sends at a metered rate.
Policing is applied outbound; shaping is applied inbound.
Policing works at Layer 2; shaping works at Layer 3.

quiz_format.explanation: **Policing** enforces a rate limit by **dropping** or **re-marking** traffic that exceeds the committed rate — it introduces no delay but causes loss. **Shaping** buffers excess traffic in a queue and transmits it at a regulated rate, introducing **delay** instead of drops. Shaping is preferred for WAN interfaces where the provider will police anyway.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's branches have 100 Mbps Ethernet handoffs to the ISP, but the contracted CIR is 50 Mbps. Traffic exceeding 50 Mbps is dropped by the ISP's policer, causing random packet loss across all applications. How should Ferguson prevent this?

options.text: Apply a policer at 50 Mbps on the branch router.
Apply traffic shaping at 50 Mbps on the branch router's egress, so QoS queuing can prioritize traffic within the shaped rate.
Request the ISP increase the policer to 100 Mbps.
Enable WRED on the branch router.

scenario_format.explanation: **Shaping** at the branch router throttles all traffic to 50 Mbps, preventing the ISP's tail-drop policer from randomly dropping packets. With shaping, Ferguson's **QoS policy (LLQ, CBWFQ)** can operate within the shaped rate, ensuring voice and critical traffic are prioritized rather than dropped indiscriminately by the ISP.

rule_format.statement: **Shape** on egress to match the provider's CIR — this enables your **QoS queuing policy** to prioritize traffic within the shaped rate, preventing the provider's policer from randomly dropping packets.

quiz_format.question: What are the three components of the Cisco MQC (Modular QoS CLI) framework?

options.text: ACL, NAT, and route-map.
Class-map (classify), policy-map (action), and service-policy (apply).
Queue, scheduler, and dropper.
Interface, VLAN, and VRF.

quiz_format.explanation: The **MQC** framework uses three elements: **class-map** (matches/classifies traffic using ACLs, DSCP, NBAR, etc.), **policy-map** (defines actions: police, shape, queue, mark), and **service-policy** (applies the policy-map to an interface in the input or output direction). This modular design allows complex QoS policies to be built and reused.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson needs to build a QoS policy that prioritizes voice, guarantees bandwidth for POS transactions, and limits recreational traffic at 1,700 branches. What framework should be used?

options.text: Legacy priority queuing with four static queues.
MQC: define class-maps for each traffic type, build a policy-map with LLQ/CBWFQ/policing actions, and apply via service-policy.
ACL-based rate-limiting on each interface.
NBAR standalone without policy-maps.

scenario_format.explanation: **MQC** provides the flexibility to define multiple **class-maps** (voice, POS, bulk, recreational), assign differentiated treatments in a **policy-map** (LLQ for voice, CBWFQ for POS, policing for recreational), and deploy consistently via **service-policy** across 1,700 branch routers using templates.

rule_format.statement: **MQC** = **class-map** (classify) + **policy-map** (act) + **service-policy** (apply). It is the standard framework for building scalable, reusable QoS policies on Cisco platforms.

quiz_format.question: What advantage does NBAR2 provide over DSCP-based classification?

options.text: It encrypts traffic based on application type.
It performs deep packet inspection to classify applications regardless of their DSCP marking, including encrypted traffic via heuristics.
It replaces QoS queuing entirely.
It only works at Layer 2.

quiz_format.explanation: **NBAR2 (Network-Based Application Recognition)** performs **deep packet inspection** and behavioral/heuristic analysis to identify applications. Unlike DSCP, which relies on the sender's marking (easily spoofed), NBAR2 can identify applications by protocol signatures, even classifying some **encrypted traffic** (e.g., identifying WebEx vs YouTube).

scenario_format.question: Ferguson branch users are watching streaming video marked DSCP 0, but it's consuming WAN bandwidth and degrading POS performance. How can the branch router identify and limit this traffic?

options.text: Block all DSCP 0 traffic.
Use NBAR2 to classify streaming video applications in a class-map, then apply policing in a policy-map.
Remark all traffic to EF at the branch.
Increase the WAN circuit bandwidth.

scenario_format.explanation: **NBAR2** can identify streaming video (Netflix, YouTube, etc.) by application signature regardless of DSCP marking. A **class-map** using `match protocol` with NBAR classifications, combined with a **policy-map** that polices or deprioritizes that class, protects POS and voice traffic without blocking legitimate business applications.

rule_format.statement: Use **NBAR2** to classify traffic by application identity (not just DSCP), enabling QoS policies that can identify and deprioritize bandwidth-heavy apps like streaming video regardless of markings.

quiz_format.question: How does WRED (Weighted Random Early Detection) differ from tail drop?

options.text: WRED drops all packets once the queue is full; tail drop drops randomly.
WRED proactively drops packets based on DSCP/IP Precedence before the queue fills, while tail drop waits until the queue is full and drops all new arrivals.
WRED only works with UDP traffic.
WRED is a queuing mechanism; tail drop is a marking mechanism.

quiz_format.explanation: **WRED** is a congestion **avoidance** mechanism that selectively drops packets based on their DSCP/IP Precedence as the queue fills. Lower-priority traffic is dropped first and more aggressively. This prevents **TCP global synchronization** where tail drop causes all TCP flows to back off simultaneously, leading to throughput oscillation.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's WAN links experience periodic throughput drops where all TCP sessions slow down simultaneously, then ramp up together, creating a sawtooth pattern. What QoS mechanism addresses this?

options.text: Strict priority queuing for all TCP traffic.
WRED on data queues to prevent TCP global synchronization by randomly dropping packets before the queue fills.
Traffic shaping at half the line rate.
Increasing the interface queue depth.

scenario_format.explanation: **TCP global synchronization** occurs when tail drop causes all flows to back off and ramp up in lockstep. **WRED** prevents this by randomly dropping packets from different flows at different times, causing independent backoff. WRED should be applied to **data queues** (not the voice LLQ) with thresholds favoring higher-DSCP traffic.

rule_format.statement: **WRED** prevents **TCP global synchronization** by randomly dropping packets before queue saturation, causing TCP flows to back off independently. Apply it to data queues, never to the voice priority queue.

quiz_format.question: What is the purpose of a QoS trust boundary in an enterprise network?

options.text: To encrypt traffic at the network perimeter.
To define the point at which DSCP/CoS markings from endpoints are accepted or overridden, preventing untrusted devices from gaming QoS policy.
To limit the number of VLANs on a switch port.
To segment the network into QoS domains.

quiz_format.explanation: The **trust boundary** is the network point where incoming DSCP/CoS markings are either trusted or re-classified. Typically, the **access layer switch** is the trust boundary — IP phones are trusted (they mark voice EF), but PC traffic behind the phone is re-marked. This prevents users from marking their traffic EF to get priority treatment.

scenario_format.question: At Ferguson branches, PCs are connected behind Cisco IP phones. A user discovers that marking their PC traffic as EF gives it priority treatment over POS transactions. How should this be prevented?

options.text: Block all EF traffic from PCs using an ACL.
Set the trust boundary at the access switch: trust DSCP from the phone's voice VLAN but re-mark PC traffic to DSCP 0 on the data VLAN.
Disable QoS on the branch router.
Use NBAR to detect spoofed DSCP markings.

scenario_format.explanation: The **trust boundary** should be the access switch port. Cisco switches support **auto-QoS** or manual trust policies that trust DSCP markings from the **voice VLAN** (phone) while re-marking data VLAN traffic to **DSCP 0**. This prevents endpoint DSCP manipulation from bypassing the QoS policy.

rule_format.statement: Set the **QoS trust boundary** at the access switch — trust markings from **IP phones** on the voice VLAN, re-mark all data VLAN traffic to prevent endpoints from spoofing DSCP values.

quiz_format.question: In a Cisco QoS policy using both LLQ and CBWFQ, what happens to the strict priority queue during congestion?

options.text: LLQ gets unlimited bandwidth and starves all other queues.
LLQ traffic is serviced first up to its configured bandwidth limit; excess is policed (dropped) to prevent starvation of CBWFQ classes.
LLQ and CBWFQ share bandwidth equally.
LLQ is disabled during congestion.

quiz_format.explanation: **LLQ** provides strict priority scheduling but includes a **built-in policer** at the configured bandwidth. Traffic exceeding the LLQ allocation is **dropped**, protecting CBWFQ classes from starvation. This is why the LLQ bandwidth should be carefully sized — typically **no more than 33%** of the link for all priority classes combined.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's branch QoS policy has LLQ for voice at 33% and CBWFQ classes for POS (20%), signaling (5%), and default (remaining). During severe congestion, can voice traffic consume more than 33%?

options.text: Yes — LLQ always takes priority regardless of allocation.
No — LLQ has a built-in policer that drops voice traffic exceeding 33%, ensuring CBWFQ classes receive their guaranteed bandwidth.
Yes — but only if the other queues are empty.
No — LLQ is converted to CBWFQ during congestion.

scenario_format.explanation: The LLQ's **implicit policer** enforces the configured bandwidth cap. Even under congestion, voice traffic exceeding 33% is **dropped**, ensuring POS (20%) and signaling (5%) receive their guaranteed allocations. This is a critical design consideration — undersize the LLQ and voice drops; oversize it and business traffic suffers.

rule_format.statement: **LLQ** = strict priority + **implicit policer**. It drops excess traffic beyond the allocated bandwidth to protect CBWFQ guarantees — size it carefully to avoid starving either voice or business-critical applications.

quiz_format.question: Why is 'qos pre-classify' needed on encrypted tunnel interfaces?

options.text: It enables hardware-based QoS on the tunnel.
After IPSec encryption, the original DSCP markings are hidden inside the encrypted payload; pre-classify preserves the inner header's markings for QoS classification.
It disables fragmentation on the tunnel.
It applies QoS before the packet enters the routing table.

quiz_format.explanation: When a packet is **IPSec encrypted**, the original IP header (with its DSCP markings) is encapsulated inside the ESP payload. Without **qos pre-classify**, the QoS policy on the physical interface only sees the outer header (which may have a generic DSCP). Pre-classify allows the policy to match against the **original inner header** before encryption.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's DMVPN branches use IPSec tunnel protection. Voice quality is poor despite a QoS policy on the physical WAN interface. Investigation shows all DMVPN traffic is classified as default. What is missing?

options.text: DSCP trust on the WAN interface.
The 'qos pre-classify' command on the tunnel interface, so the QoS policy can see the original DSCP markings before encryption.
A dedicated voice VLAN on the WAN interface.
NBAR on the tunnel interface.

scenario_format.explanation: After **IPSec encryption**, the original DSCP values are hidden inside the ESP payload. The QoS policy on the physical interface sees only the outer GRE/IPSec header. Enabling **qos pre-classify** on the tunnel interface tells IOS to save the original classification for use by the egress QoS policy.

rule_format.statement: Enable **qos pre-classify** on IPSec/GRE tunnel interfaces — without it, encryption hides the inner DSCP markings and the physical interface's QoS policy classifies all tunnel traffic as default.

quiz_format.question: What is a Cisco Nexus vPC (Virtual Port Channel)?

options.text: A protocol that creates a virtual chassis from two physical switches.
A technology that allows a downstream device to form a port channel across two independent Nexus switches, eliminating STP blocked ports.
A VPN tunneling protocol for data center interconnect.
A virtual routing instance on a single Nexus switch.

quiz_format.explanation: **vPC** allows two Nexus switches to appear as a single logical port-channel endpoint to downstream devices (servers, switches). This eliminates **STP blocked ports**, doubles available bandwidth, and provides sub-second failover. The two switches synchronize state via a **peer link** and **peer keepalive** channel.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's DC has paired Nexus 9000 switches at the aggregation layer. Servers need active-active dual-homing without STP blocking any uplinks. What technology should be deployed?

options.text: Spanning-tree with RPVST+ for per-VLAN load balancing.
vPC — servers bond NICs into a port channel that spans both Nexus switches, providing active-active forwarding with no STP blocked ports.
MLAG using standard IOS commands.
FabricPath for Layer 2 multi-pathing.

scenario_format.explanation: **vPC** enables the server to form a standard LACP port channel with members on two separate Nexus switches. Both links are **active-active** (no STP blocking), and if one switch fails, traffic converges to the surviving switch within **milliseconds** via the vPC peer link.

rule_format.statement: **vPC** presents two physical Nexus switches as one logical port-channel endpoint — this enables **active-active dual-homing**, eliminates STP blocked ports, and provides sub-second failover.

question_text: vPC peer link and keepalive roles

quiz_format.question: What is the difference between the vPC peer link and the vPC peer keepalive in a Nexus vPC domain?

options.text: The peer link carries data traffic; the keepalive carries routing protocol updates.
The peer link synchronizes MAC tables, ARP, and carries orphan port traffic; the keepalive is a heartbeat that detects peer failure to prevent split-brain.
The peer link is optional; the keepalive is mandatory.
They serve the same function and are interchangeable.

quiz_format.explanation: The **peer link** (typically a 10G+ port channel) synchronizes **MAC/ARP tables** and carries BUM (Broadcast, Unknown unicast, Multicast) traffic plus orphan port traffic. The **peer keepalive** (a lightweight L3 heartbeat, often over the management VRF) detects peer liveness and prevents **dual-active (split-brain)** scenarios.

scenario_format.question: Ferguson's Nexus vPC pair loses the peer link but the keepalive remains up. What happens to the vPC secondary switch?

options.text: It becomes the primary switch.
The secondary suspends its vPC member ports to prevent duplicate traffic, since the keepalive confirms the primary is still alive.
Both switches continue forwarding normally.
All traffic is dropped on both switches.

scenario_format.explanation: When the **peer link fails** but the **keepalive remains up**, the secondary knows the primary is still alive. To prevent **split-brain** (both switches forwarding independently), the secondary **suspends its vPC member ports**. If the keepalive also failed, the secondary would assume the primary is dead and keep its ports active.

rule_format.statement: Peer link down + keepalive up = **secondary suspends vPC ports** to prevent split-brain. The keepalive's role is to detect whether the peer is alive when the peer link is lost.

quiz_format.question: How does the NX-OS feature enablement model differ from traditional IOS?

options.text: NX-OS loads all features by default; IOS requires manual enablement.
NX-OS requires explicit 'feature <name>' commands to enable protocols (OSPF, BGP, vPC, etc.); IOS includes most features by default.
NX-OS uses a license file for each feature; IOS does not.
There is no difference; both platforms work identically.

quiz_format.explanation: NX-OS uses a **modular feature architecture** where protocols are not available until explicitly enabled (e.g., `feature ospf`, `feature bgp`, `feature vpc`). This reduces memory usage and attack surface. In traditional IOS, most protocols are available by default. Forgetting `feature <name>` is a common NX-OS misconfiguration.

scenario_format.question: A Ferguson engineer configures OSPF on a new Nexus 9000 switch and receives 'Invalid command.' What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The OSPF license is not installed.
The 'feature ospf' command has not been executed — NX-OS requires explicit feature enablement before protocol commands are available.
OSPF is not supported on Nexus 9000.
The switch needs a reboot after initial setup.

scenario_format.explanation: NX-OS requires **feature ospf** before any OSPF configuration commands become available. This is the **modular feature enablement** model unique to NX-OS. Common features that must be enabled include: **ospf, bgp, vpc, hsrp, lacp, interface-vlan, pim**, and **nv overlay** for VXLAN.

rule_format.statement: NX-OS uses **explicit feature enablement** — run `feature <protocol>` before configuring any protocol. This reduces resource consumption and attack surface but catches engineers used to IOS off guard.

quiz_format.question: What is a VDC (Virtual Device Context) on a Nexus 7000?

options.text: A VPN routing instance similar to a VRF.
